Whazzup Email Player,
I once talked about the following on a podcast my droogJim Yaghi hosted, and got
in a wee bit of trouble from some of the lady folk afterwards. They were NOT amused
with your humble host. And, some were quite tiüd with me. But, so be it. I am not one to
back down from controversy (and, neither should you, if you want to make the maximum
sales in your emails). And, the few who confronted me about it eventually admitted I was
right (when are y'awl gonna realize I am ALWAYS right? And that, while my opinion
may change, the fact I am always right never chan§es??)when I made them think past
their initial annoyance at me (and, really, I couldn't blame 'em) and walked them through
what I am about to explain here. Anyway, what set'em all off at me is when I said the
best way to attract and keep good customers and clients is to "pi*p" them... just like a
smart man will do to attract and keep a good woman in his life. Yes, I realize this is not
'politically correct." And, I will explain what I mean by this. But first, let's take off the
politica\ correct goggles for a moment and get down to the root of human nature, men
and women, seller and buyer. Sometimes confronting uncomfortable truths and facts is
the only way to groq expand your horizons and, ultimately, make more of the green stuff
in ye olde business (especially with email). Ary*uy, here is what I mean by "pimping"
your market:

Pimping is kind of a crude way of sayng, you have to basically treat your best
customers ándclients and prospects like you would treat a hot chick you want to date. By
that I mean, hot chicks are used to being hit on everywhere they go (even to the point
where many I know wear wedding rings to discourage men from constantly hitting on
them). Th.y are fawned over. When they post a picture on Flakebook, all the desperate
and áeedy guys will "like" it and comment on how beautiful she is, etc. When she is
pulled o*. foi speeding, she is more often than not let off with a warning (somehow
that's not considered sexist in this backasswards culture)'
She is used to being the center of attention.
She is surrounded by guys who "orbit" her, always doing whatever she asks in
hopes she will notice them and give them her affection (which she never does).
And, most important of all for purposes of email marketing... she is NOT used
to the bitter stingLf rejection! Nor does she have to ever talk her way into haüng sex
*itf, u guy or getting a date. She simply has to "be" and guys will flock to her and she can
pick aná choose whá she wants. And the guy who stands out in her world is not the
j,OOgth dude to like her selfie pic of when she woke up in the morning and is looking for
aitention. No, the dude who sticks out is the guy who comments on said photo with
something like, "is that a booger hanging from your nose? Gross." It's also üe guy who
dares cancel a date on her at the last minute. Who flirts with other girls in front of her-
And, who basica\ puts himself and his interests first, and treats her like he is "better"
than her, with an air of superiority where she knows he always has one foot out the door
and she does not "have" him.
In other words... he's created rnofe VALUE than the other guys.
He is not just another orbiter begging for attention.
He is someone who has her chasing him, and not the other way around like it is
üth all the oüer guys orbiting her. This makes her want him, think only about him, and
not be satisfied üth anyone else.That, my fine feathered little droogie, is what I mean by
"pimping." It's not atactícor something done maliciously, necessarily (although some
guys do it that way, I suppose). It's simply a natural byproduct of this particular guy
k owing his value (or thinking has more value than he really has), haüng an abundance
mentality (in sales we call this "posture") and liüng his life by his rules, not letting her and

And the list goes on...
You have to constantly "pimp" your market like this.
Iike with a hot chick who is constantly testing you for your manhood... you cannot
just love a client or customer who is always being "courted" by your competition. You
have to love them and, at times if necessary (and it's not always necessary)be a bit of an
asshole for their own good. This does NOT mean being a dick or cruel or an obnoxious
idiot who insults people. It's about being in charge of yourself and your life and liüng by
a code of ethics and personal dignity you don't let others alter or bend. (This will almost
always make some people think you are an asshole, even though you're not.) And, again,
it's no different than if you're a guy wanting the affections of a hot woman who is fawned
over by a gazillion guys (just as the best customers and clients are fawned over b)¡ all the
marketers competing for their attention)who have no idea how to play the game,
withhold interest if their time or generosity is taken for granted... and focus on being the
best at what they do, putting themselves first. By doing that, happiness and success with
"trickle" down from you to the client or customer. It cannot happen the other way around
or they wil (rightfully) find someone else other than you to buy from. And here's the
kicker with all üis:
Your customers and clients WANT you to do this.
They WANT you to pimp them, even if they'll never admit it.
They do not want a pushover or mush cookie who they can easily manipulate and
bend to their will or power trips any more than a woman wants a man like that.
So not only are you doing what's in YOUR best interest... you're doing what's in
your client and customer's best interest, too. Hey, nobody said pimpin, was easy.
But, if you do it right, it is definitely profitable...

on tracking open rates despite the stats being ürtually worthless, then pay ye attention:
Sup Ben,
I know you've been saying that for a while, but now we got proof:
http: / /www. dropdeadcor¡y. com/wp-content,/uploads /20 13 /09 /vishen-lakhiani-
screenshot-2 -copv. pnq

http : / /www. dropdeadcor¡y . com/vishen- lakhiani-on-mindval leys - f unne 1-
analvs is -model-how-to-write-the-oer f eet-ema i 1- senrr en r:e /

And as an interesting addendum to that email... here is what my droog, computer
scientist and AdWords "wizard"Jim Yaghi said when I showed it to him about his rlsults:
bro, i notice that emails with LOw open rates and,/or LOlf click-rates
tend to sell best for me. Not that i intentionally try to get low open
and click rates...it just "so happens" and since i don't care about
either, it just depends on my luck that day haha.
I am not surprised SirJim of Castle Yaghi. Not. One. Bit. The real power in email
is NOT wasting time tracking a bunch of soft metrics (i.e. any metric that isn't a sale). It's
in relentlessly emailing day after da)r. The real power is in repetition. In consistently
sending them every day. And, in having the right kind of conversation with your market.
(Atl of which I teach in the hallowed pages of this newsletter each month.) Don't let the
goo-roos intimidate you. Don't let them lie to you about their "testing stats" which, even
if true, are 1007o irrelevant to you, your market and your product. And, don't let them
paralyze you with a testing complex. Focus on writing emails regularly. On doing them
right (so people look forward to getting them). And, on tracking sales trends over time --
not haüng a cow over open rates on any given day (which, as you've seen, can often be
LOWER on days your sales are higher).
The rest will take care of itself ...

And that's the point I want to leave you wiü here:
If someone compares you to a popular TV character or author or some kind of
well-known personality... MILK IT! Make sure you ask permission to use that testimonial
in your .*rlr, on your sites, sales letters, etc. And, also, as a tip: Make them short,
"sóund byte" like testimonials. For example: Roger Haeske's Seinfeld testimonial was a
long email going into detail about why he called me the Seinfeld of email. But, it would
losJimpact if I posted the entire thing on my testimonials page. Instead, I pulled the
sound bye out ánd used that. If you are building an entire email around a comparison
testimonial then it's fine to reprint the whole thing. In fact, I suggest making that
testimonial the email (with you gürg an intro and call to action afterwards), and then
using the "sound byte" portion in the email subject line.
So in my case I did this:
Subject line: The Seinfeld of email

When sitting next to someone on the plane, and they have the arm
rest, you tuck your elbows in a little differently, so you can
quickly slide your arm on the rest as fast as possible. And, what
you do is, you wait until he scratches his nose or rubs his eyes or
does some other gesture where he momentarily raises his arm.
And then...
You steal the arm rest for the rest of the flight!
(Extra points if you smirk a wink at 'em right after...)
Anl¡ray, good advice for them looooong flights.
And you know what?
It's also good advice for email marketing.
ft's actually a lot how my email system works.
Here's what f mean:
When you have people on your list ¡¿ho are also on your competitor's
Iist, you are essentially sharing an arm rest with them. And if
your competitor has better positioning, personal branding, a bigger
list, more subscribers, a rock star presence in your market, and so
forth he basically has the arm rest.
üIhat that means is:
He's comfy.
And, you ain't.
But, guess what?
Eventually, they'11 stop mailing for a few days.
(Or weeks. )
They'11 get Lazy.
Or, just take their list for granted.
In other words...

double their sales in the next 30 days:
L.) Quit pounding people with JUST straight offers
2.) TaIk up the benefits of buying food by mait
3.) Give helpful tips about cooking steaks and other meals,
nutritional facts, show how protein melts away flab, recipe eBooks,
etc (a11 related to Omaha Steaks' products, of course...)
4. ) Have a personality "writing', the emails
5. ) Don't make everything look so commercial (test using prain text
or HTML that looks like plain text -- i.e. Gary Halbert,s "A pile/B
pile" theory applied to email)
Look, I know Omaha Steaks ain't listening to this.
And who knows?
Maybe they tested this stuff already.
I don't much care, either way.
What I care about is you...
Yada, yada,yada, then I went into my call to action.
I also did this to Dodge after their lame 2013 Superbowl ad. (lMhich got me the
attention of a hater, who wrote me some entertaining emails I was able to use as "fodder"
for more emails... see, emails beget emails, never fo'get...) Anyway, a great little format
